sections.heading: What are ViewGroups?
sections.content: ViewGroups are containers that hold and arrange other Views. They control how child Views are measured and positioned on screen. Choosing the right layout helps keep the UI efficient and easy to maintain.

sections.heading: LinearLayout
sections.content: LinearLayout arranges its child Views in a single direction: vertical (top to bottom) or horizontal (side by side). It is simple and ideal for basic forms or rows of items.

sections.heading: Important LinearLayout Attributes
items: android:gravity – aligns all children inside layout
android:layout_weight – distributes available space among views

sections.heading: ConstraintLayout
sections.content: ConstraintLayout is the recommended layout for modern Android apps. It positions views using constraints between other views or the parent, allowing complex UIs with a flat hierarchy.

sections.heading: Key Features of ConstraintLayout
items: Flat layout hierarchy for better performance
Supports chains, guidelines, and bias for flexible layouts

rows.purpose: Align top to parent or another view

rows.description: Space inside the view around its content
Space outside the view between other views

sections.heading: When to Use Each Layout
items: Use LinearLayout for simple stacked layouts
Use FrameLayout for fragments or overlapping views

sections.heading: Best Practices
items: Prefer ConstraintLayout to reduce nested layouts
Use start/end instead of left/right for RTL support
Use resource files for strings, colors, and dimensions

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: ConstraintLayout and LinearLayout are the most common Android layouts. LinearLayout is simple and ideal for basic rows or columns, while ConstraintLayout is powerful and efficient for complex responsive screens.
